name: Electrical Telegraph
tagline: Pulses on wires encoding letters at light-speed — shrinking command-and-control latency across continents
problem:
Horse couriers and semaphore chains could not keep pace with rail timetables, naval logistics, or commodity prices. The telegraph encodes language into electrical events — closing circuits for dots and dashes — so operators transcribe at human speed while propagation delay is negligible on land.

Challenges include insulation on long buried or submarine cables, lightning induction burning instruments, and synchronising clocks for train safety. Codebooks compress proprietary business messages; cryptography appears immediately.

Economically, telegraphy priced information like freight — but by the bit, not the ton.
overview:
A battery keys a line; an electromagnet pulls a sounder or marks a paper tape. Repeater stations regenerate weak signals. Insulators of glass or porcelain separate wires from earth; lightning arrestors sacrifice paths to protect offices.

Submarine cables add gutta-percha or polyethylene jackets, armouring, and repeaters in fibre era — but copper coax ancestors carried telegraphy across oceans first with heroic thickness and regretful engineering guesses about capacitance.

Morse code standardises timing; continental variants tweak alphabet mappings. Training schools produce operators with rhythmic ears — a craft killed by telephone speech then resurrected as CW ham radio.

Telegraph offices became nodes in graphs; stock tickers and train order offices were clients — infrastructure thinking we now call networking.

principles.name: Ohm’s law on long lines
principles.explanation: Leakage conductance and capacitance smear pulses — Heaviside’s loading coils and later repeaters fight distortion.

principles.name: Signal-to-noise in wet insulation
principles.explanation: Moisture raises conductance — maintenance crews walk lines with megohmmeters.

principles.name: Human baud rate
principles.explanation: Skilled Morse beats naive typing for skinny channels — ergonomics of rhythm.

principles.name: Common versus metallic circuit
principles.explanation: Early earth returns save copper; interference teaches symmetric pairs.

buildSteps.title: Survey right-of-way
buildSteps.description: Negotiate poles with landowners; map river crossings.
buildSteps.warningNote: Induced liability from fallen wires — tension engineering

buildSteps.title: String and sag
buildSteps.description: Match mechanical tension to wind and ice load; bond grounds.
buildSteps.warningNote: Ground potential differences shock animals tied to wet earth returns

buildSteps.title: Install protection
buildSteps.description: Lightning horns; fuses; office grounding mats.
buildSteps.warningNote: Nearby lightning magnetises sounders — false clicks

buildSteps.title: Train operators
buildSteps.description: Copy qualification tests; procedural language for train orders.

buildSteps.title: Maintain repeaters
buildSteps.description: Log battery voltages; clean relay contacts; document drift.

history.event: Patent demonstrations of practical electromagnetic telegraphs in UK and USA.
history.person: William Fothergill Cooke, Charles Wheatstone, Samuel Morse

history.event: Successful transatlantic telegraph cable after earlier failures.

inventors.name: Samuel Morse
inventors.contribution: Practical telegraph instrument and Morse code co-development

impact:
Telegraphy coordinated empires and markets in real time — shrinking strategic surprise while raising the tempo of war and finance.

It is the ancestor of every packet — meaning encoded as physical state on a wire.
